story:
Waxman had tracked the Arak for weeks across the dun, wind-scoured plains of the Koutar, reading its patterns of flight, waiting for twilight’s soft, golden hush. At last, on a bleak plateau of fractured rock, he climbed through thorn and bracken to a narrow ledge that looked over the endless country below. There he found the nest. A score of eggs lay in a shallow cup of kirrel grass, each a marvel—stones in a jeweler’s tray, no two alike. A delicate coffee shell freckled with gold. A small egg the color of daybreak, a blue so tentative it seemed certain to fade with the sun. They dazzled him, set his heart racing, and reminded him why he had come.

He had not come to take them all. Tradition and hard experience said that only one truly mattered—the Alpha prime. The rest, for all their beauty, would hatch the lesser brood: betas, echoes of the great predator, fit for the yard or the pot, never for the clean, blazing arc of a pure Arak’s hunt. Waxman fought the urge to seize the first egg his hand touched and run before the parent returned. He forced himself to weigh and watch, to read the shells like signs.

He tested a perfect violet egg, twilight caught in an ellipse: all form, balance, and harmony, as though flight itself had hardened into shell. Surely form and function were one—logic said so. Yet another egg drew him on: a scoured tan, plain and heavy, holding much and saying little. The Arak was like that too—ferocity and sinew, yes, but also reserve and secrecy, something held back, something unknown. Logic, he saw, would not reveal the Alpha. The nest’s wild variety—so many eggs, so many clues—might be camouflage, or a kind of defiance: a burst of color against the Koutar’s barren sameness, a way for a living thing to answer emptiness with abundance. Given time, he thought, he could read these shells as a scholar reads books, and build a portrait of the Arak entire.

A tremor in the air made him freeze: the whisper of great wings. He dropped low, cloak over his head, pulse hammering. Crawling for cover, his hand brushed an egg unlike the others—ivory with a rose blush, marked by a deep V near the crown. The mark did not fit his idea of what an egg should be; it seemed to belong to another order of birth altogether, like a scar where a cord once bound life to life. His mind flared. This one. He cradled it, and felt the faint beat within answering his own. In that moment he understood how narrow he had been: the Arak was more than a bird. In patience and cunning, in ruthless purpose, it resembled Man—and, in this burned land, had risen by combining the instincts of two predators into a single will.

The Arak returned, a shadow at the ledge’s end, eyes bright and unreadable. Waxman knew much of its flight and hunting, little of what bound it to a mate or to a nest. Stiff-legged, the great creature advanced, and the plateau filled with a pressure that brooked no argument. She fell upon him in a flurry of wings, reclaiming what was hers, and the Koutar carried his cries into the dark as night closed over them both.
